title: Superconductivity-Enabled Conversion of Ferromagnetic Resonance into Standing Spin Waves
authors: Ya. V. Turkin, N. G. Pugach, F. M. Maksimov, A. S. Pakhomov, A. I. Chernov, V. I. Belotelov, S. N. Polulyakh, V. S. Stolyarov
abstract: Superconductors can transport spin without Joule dissipation, yet their coherent coupling to short-wavelength magnons in insulating magnets remains largely unexplored. Here we demonstrate experimentally and theoretically that a conventional diffusive superconductor can enable the conversion of the uniform ferromagnetic-resonance (FMR) mode into perpendicular standing spin waves (PSSWs) in an adjacent ferrimagnetic insulator. In Bi-substituted iron-garnet/Nb bilayers, the microwave transmission develops an additional resonance feature that appears only below the Nb transition temperature and lies close to the uniform FMR peak. A microscopic theory that self-consistently couples the quasiclassical Keldysh--Usadel description of the superconducting condensate to the Landau--Lifshitz--Gilbert dynamics shows that the conversion requires two ingredients: (i) an interfacial spin-transfer torque mediated by spin-polarized triplet Cooper pairs and (ii) a depth-dependent effective field produced by Abrikosov vortices (electromagnetic proximity). The resulting susceptibility reproduces the measured lineshapes and establishes superconductivity as an active control knob for exchange standing-wave modes in magnetic insulators.
summary: The researchers show that a superconductor can act as a control knob to convert uniform magnetic resonance into standing spin waves in an adjacent insulator. This process is driven by spin-transfer torque from triplet Cooper pairs and electromagnetic effects from superconducting vortices. This discovery opens new ways to manipulate magnonic modes using superconducting states.
main_problem: Exploring the coherent coupling between a diffusive superconductor and short-wavelength magnons in an adjacent insulating magnet to enable mode conversion.
main_result: Demonstrated that superconductivity can convert uniform ferromagnetic resonance (FMR) into perpendicular standing spin waves (PSSWs) via spin-polarized triplet Cooper pairs and electromagnetic proximity from Abrikceptov vortices.
method: Experimental microwave transmission measurements on bilayers combined with a microscopic theory coupling the Keldysh-Usadel description of superconductivity to Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert dynamics.
model_or_system: Bi-substituted iron-garnet/Nb bilayers consisting of a ferrimagnetic insulator adjacent to a conventional diffusive superconductor.
key_observables: Microwave transmission resonance features and magnetic susceptibility lineshapes.
important_parameters: Nb transition temperature, spin-polarized triplet Cooper pair-mediated torque, and Abrikosov vortex-induced effective field.
main_assumptions: The superconductor is treated as a conventional diffusive system described by the Keldysh-Usadel formalism.
paper_structure: The paper presents an experimental demonstration of FMR to PSSW conversion in bilayers, followed by a microscopic theoretical derivation using Keldysh-Usadel and LLG dynamics to explain the mechanism.
